issues.description: The function returns the wrong expression. The root node is popped and processed first (stack is LIFO), so its result is at index 0 of `results`. But `results.last()` returns the result of the last-processed node, which is a leaf/child — not the root. For example, if the root is a binary_expression, its result goes into results[0], then the right child's result into results[1], then the left child's into results[2]. `last()` returns the left child instead of the root binary expression.
issues.suggested_fix: Return `results.into_iter().next().flatten()` (the first result) if keeping this approach, or redesign to use `results.first()`. However, this alone doesn't fix the deeper design issues — see other findings.

issues.description: The binary_expression arm both pushes children onto the stack for later iterative processing AND calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` which recursively processes those same children. This causes: (1) each child subtree is processed twice — once recursively inside lower_binary_expr and once when popped from the stack; (2) the extra child results are appended to `results`, corrupting the result vector and making the final `last()` return a child's result instead of the root's.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the `stack.push(child)` loop inside the binary_expression arm. If falling back to recursive `lower_binary_expr`, don't also push children. Alternatively, implement a proper two-phase iterative algorithm (push frames, then reconstruct on pop) and remove the recursive fallback.

issues.description: The method's doc comment says it avoids stack overflow on deeply nested expressions, but it falls back to full recursion for every compound expression type: binary_expression calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` (which recurses via lower_expr), unary_expression calls `self.lower_unary_expr(node)` (recurses via lower_expr), function_call calls `self.lower_function_call(node)` (recurses via lower_expr), and the catch-all `_` arm calls `self.lower_expr(node)` directly. A deeply nested binary expression tree (the most common deeply-nested case) will still overflow the stack because lower_binary_expr → lower_expr → lower_binary_expr chains recursively.
issues.suggested_fix: A correct iterative lowering requires a two-pass approach: (1) a work-stack that decomposes nodes into pending operations (e.g., 'lower left child, then lower right child, then combine into BinOp'), and (2) a result-stack where completed sub-expressions are consumed by parent operations. The current flat Vec<Option<Expr>> with no structural bookkeeping cannot reconstruct tree-shaped AST nodes from their children.

issues.description: The parenthesized_expression arm pushes children and `continue`s, skipping the `results.push(expr)` at the bottom of the loop. If a parenthesized expression appears as a child of a binary expression that was processed via recursive fallback, the paren child will be popped from the stack, its inner expression pushed again, and eventually a result lands in `results` — but disconnected from any parent. The filter `child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` is also redundant since `(` and `)` are anonymous tokens and already excluded by `child.is_named()`.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the redundant kind checks. For the structural issue, this requires the same two-phase iterative redesign mentioned above.

issues.description: The method `lower_expr_iterative` is not called anywhere in the codebase. It is dead code that will trigger a compiler warning (unused method) unless it has a `#[allow(dead_code)]` attribute or is called from somewhere not yet committed.
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire up callers (e.g., have lower_expr delegate to lower_expr_iterative for deeply nested trees based on a depth threshold) or remove the method until it is needed.

opus_bug_hunter.summary: The new `lower_expr_iterative` method has three blocking correctness bugs. First, it returns the wrong expression: `results.last()` returns a leaf node's result instead of the root, because the root is processed first (LIFO stack) and its result is at index 0. Second, the binary_expression arm both pushes children for iterative processing AND recursively processes them via `lower_binary_expr`, causing double processing and corrupting the results vector. Third, the method fundamentally fails to achieve its stated goal of preventing stack overflow — every compound expression type (binary, unary, function call, and the catch-all) falls back to full recursion through `lower_expr`, so a deeply nested binary expression tree will still overflow. A correct iterative AST lowering requires a two-phase work-stack/result-stack algorithm to decompose nodes and reconstruct tree structure; the current flat `Vec<Option<Expr>>` approach cannot represent parent-child relationships. The method is also currently dead code with no callers.

issues.description: The iterative approach is fundamentally broken. It collects results into a flat Vec<Option<Expr>> but the AST is tree-structured (Expr contains Box<Expr> children). There is no mechanism to reassemble parent nodes from their children's results. A correct iterative tree-lowering requires either a two-stack approach (work stack + result stack with frame markers/continuations) or a CPS transform. The current code pushes children onto the work stack but then has no way to wire their results back into the parent Expr node — `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` just returns whatever was processed last, which is arbitrary and almost certainly wrong for any non-leaf expression.
issues.suggested_fix: Either implement a proper two-phase iterative algorithm with an explicit result stack and frame markers (enum WorkItem { Process(Node), Combine { kind, arity }}) or, if deep nesting is rare, use stacker::maybe_grow() to extend the call stack on demand while keeping the clean recursive design.

issues.description: The binary_expression arm pushes named children onto the stack for later processing AND immediately calls self.lower_binary_expr(node), which itself recursively calls lower_expr on the same children (lines 1729-1730 of the existing code). This produces duplicate work: children are lowered recursively via lower_binary_expr now, AND will be popped from the stack and processed again later, appending spurious results to the results vec. The comment acknowledges the problem ('Can't construct BinaryExpr yet') but the fallback makes it worse, not better.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the stack.push(child) calls in the binary_expression arm if falling back to recursive lowering, or implement proper deferred construction if doing true iterative processing.

issues.description: The parenthesized_expression arm pushes children onto the stack and continues (skipping result collection), but the outer code expects results.last() to be the root expression's result. If the root node is a parenthesized_expression, it produces no result itself — the inner child's result ends up in results, which coincidentally works for a single-level unwrap but fails for nested parenthesized expressions or when parenthesized_expression is a sub-tree of a larger expression that was also pushed.
issues.suggested_fix: A correct iterative parenthesized-expression unwrap should iteratively peel parentheses without using the stack, or the stack protocol must properly propagate child results to parent frames.

issues.description: lower_expr_iterative is never called anywhere in the codebase. It is dead code with no call site in the diff or existing code. Adding untested, uncalled methods increases maintenance burden and risks someone calling it later and getting silent wrong results.
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire this method into the call sites that need it (replacing lower_expr where deep nesting is a concern) and add tests, or remove it until a correct implementation is ready.

issues.description: Most arms (number_literal, string_literal, bool_literal, identifier, unary_expression, function_call, and the _ fallback) simply delegate to the same recursive helpers that lower_expr uses, meaning they still recurse through lower_expr for any sub-expressions. The method is iterative in name only — it doesn't eliminate recursion for any expression type except parenthesized_expression. This defeats the stated purpose of 'avoiding stack overflow on deeply nested expressions'.
issues.suggested_fix: If the goal is stack overflow protection, consider using the stacker crate (stacker::maybe_grow) as a minimal wrapper around lower_expr. This preserves the clean recursive design while dynamically growing the stack only when needed, and is a common pattern in Rust compilers (rustc uses it).

issues.description: The check `child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` is redundant. Parenthesis tokens are unnamed nodes in tree-sitter, so `child.is_named()` already excludes them. The extra string comparisons add noise without filtering anything additional.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the `&& child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` clause; `is_named()` is sufficient.

opus_design_critic.summary: The new lower_expr_iterative method is architecturally unsound and algorithmically incorrect. Its core flaw is attempting to flatten a tree-structured AST lowering into a simple stack+flat-results-vec pattern, which cannot reconstruct parent Expr nodes from their children's results. The binary_expression arm exemplifies the confusion: it pushes children for later stack processing but simultaneously calls the recursive lower_binary_expr, causing duplicate processing. Most other arms simply delegate to the existing recursive helpers, making the method iterative in name only. The method is also dead code with no call site. If stack overflow on deep nesting is a real concern, the idiomatic Rust solution is stacker::maybe_grow() around the existing recursive lower_expr, preserving the clean dispatch pattern that the rest of the parser (48 call sites, 17+ recursive helpers) relies on.
